title: Diagnostics, partitions, and bayesplot interoperability
text: two questions one usually asks clustering posterior, need different summaries. “component parameters?” needs labels aligned across draws, relabel() conditioning modal number occupied clusters applying label-switching algorithm. Conditioning unavoidable – draw four clusters “third component” align draw three – means draws set aside: half draws sit modal K = 3; rest inform K partition per-component summaries. “observations belong together?” question need labels , co-clustering label-invariant: whether observations j share component depend component called. psm() estimates posterior similarity matrix Sij=Pr⁡(zi=zj∣y)S_{ij} = \Pr(z_i = z_j \mid y) every draw, binderPartition() returns point partition minimising expected Binder (1978) loss – equivalently, Dahl’s (2006) least-squares criterion, searched partitions chain actually visited. relabel() call needed, draws discarded. two tools complements: use relabel() component parameters, psm()/ binderPartition() partition. overlapping clusters similarity matrix especially informative, reports genuine allocation uncertainty (entries near 0.5) instead forcing hard assignment.
code: Kpost <- apply(fit@clusterAllocation, 1, function(z) length(unique(z))) table(Kpost) #> Kpost #>    3    4    5    6    7    8 #> 1215  748  317   93   21    6 S  <- psm(fit) bp <- binderPartition(fit, S) bp$K #> [1] 3 table(bp$partition) #> #>   1   2   3 #> 118  98 114

what: Convergence diagnostics that are actually meaningful
title: Diagnostics, partitions, and bayesplot interoperability
text: Reaching R-hat muTilde[1] trap: label switching trace refers different components different chains, number looks plausible means nothing. nimix steers label-invariant functionals – number occupied clusters, allocation entropy, (DPM fits) concentration alpha – meaningful raw draws retain per-chain structure R-hat needs. drawsArray() returns iterations x chains x parameters layout bayesplot’s mcmc_* functions accept natively: Ask component parameters relabelling adaptor refuses, purpose: fit <- relabel(fit) component view available; relabelling conditions modal K, chains longer equal lengths, chain dimension honestly collapsed one – fine posterior density interval plots, cross-chain R-hat.
code: da <- drawsArray(fit)                 # params = "invariant" by default dim(da) #> [1] 1200    2    3 dimnames(da)[[3]] #> [1] "K"       "entropy" "alpha" drawsArray(fit, "components") #> Error: drawsArray(params = "components") needs relabel() first. #>   Component draws are not identified before relabelling: ...

title: Diagnostics, partitions, and bayesplot interoperability
text: bayesplot sits Suggests. adaptors return base R objects, nothing pulled namespace load bayesplot . ppcData() wraps posteriorPredict(), returns replicated data sets (ndraws x n matrix, ndraws x n x d multivariate fits). numeric summary rather plot, ppCheck() gives tail-area posterior predictive p-values set statistics: Values away 0 1 indicate replicated data reproduce statistic observed data – four comfortably interior.
code: library(bayesplot)  # convergence on the label-invariant functionals mcmc_trace(drawsArray(fit)) mcmc_rhat_hist(rhat(drawsArray(fit)))  # posterior predictive overlay pd <- ppcData(fit, ndraws = 50)       # list(y, yrep) ppc_dens_overlay(pd$y, pd$yrep) ppCheck(fit, nrep = 200) #>   statistic  observed  repMean   ppp #> 1      mean    ...      ...      0.490 #> 2        sd    ...      ...      0.710 #> 3       min    ...      ...      0.130 #> 4       max    ...      ...      0.735

what: Every plot hands back its data
title: Diagnostics, partitions, and bayesplot interoperability
text: built-plot() methods draw base graphics return, invisibly, tidy data frame plotted – can reproduce restyle ggplot2, lattice, plotly without nimix depending packages.
code: d <- plot(fit, type = "density")      # draws the base plot head(d)                               # ... and returns x / density library(ggplot2) ggplot(d, aes(x, density)) + geom_line()

what: A secondary lens: internal validity indices
title: Diagnostics, partitions, and bayesplot interoperability
text: clusterValidity() bridges Binder partition classical internal indices – silhouette width (cluster), Dunn Calinski-Harabasz (fpc), Suggests-: One caveat matters enough repeat. indices reward geometric separation; mixture density-based. fit genuinely overlapping components – often scientifically correct model – scores low: Low silhouette evidence model – ppCheck() fit clean. Use indices compare partitions (say, across candidate K) equal footing, keep model adequacy questions ppCheck() psm(). full battery beyond three, backends accept pair function assembles: fpc::cluster.stats(dist(fit@data), binderPartition(fit)$partition).
code: clusterValidity(fit) #> silhouette       dunn         ch #>      0.903      1.616   4299.717      # well-separated example # two components centred at -0.7 and +0.7: a legitimate model of this data clusterValidity(fit_overlap) #> silhouette       dunn         ch #>      0.522      0.000    224.123

title: Regime switching in time series: the hidden-Markov engine
text: data order time order – Markov prior binds .
code: library(nimix)  set.seed(11) P  <- rbind(c(0.95, 0.05),    # regime 1: very persistent             c(0.10, 0.90))    # regime 2: persistent mu <- c(-2, 2); sg <- c(0.6, 0.8) z  <- integer(300); z[1] <- 1 for (t in 2:300) z[t] <- sample(1:2, 1, prob = P[z[t - 1], ]) y  <- rnorm(300, mu[z], sg[z])

title: Regime switching in time series: the hidden-Markov engine
text: K number states (fixed, like method = "fixedk"); current emission families univariate Gaussian, Student-t (heavy-tailed regimes financial returns), Poisson (regime-switching counts), Binomial (regime-switching proportions known number trials, via prior = list(size = ); measured recovery 0.150/0.597 truth 0.15/0.6 Viterbi 1.0), nine neo-normal / skew families: MSNBurr, MSNBurr-IIa, GMSNBurr, FSSN, FSST, SEP, LEP, FOSSEP, JFST – thirteen , completing planned univariate set. FSST JFST give regimes skewed heavy-tailed , often asset returns actually look like; SEP/LEP’s tail-shape parameter well identified practice (measured 1.30/3.48 truth 1.2/3.5). normal-gamma excluded design – augmented representation exactly marginalised forward kernel avoids, direct Student-t serves heavy-tail case. estimated persistence read straight transition draws:
code: fit <- nimixClust(y, K = 2, method = "hmm",                   mcmcControl = list(niter = 4000, nburnin = 1500,                                      nchains = 2),                   seed = 1)  fit <- relabel(fit) fit@relabeled$summary[, c("mu_mean", "s2_mean")] #>   mu_mean s2_mean #> 1   -1.99   0.432 #> 2    2.12   0.868      # truth: mu -2, 2; s2 0.36, 0.64 mean(fit@mcmcSamples[, "P[1, 1]"]) #> [1] 0.965                          # truth 0.95; 95% CI [0.936, 0.985] mean(fit@mcmcSamples[, "P[2, 2]"]) #> [1] 0.896                          # truth 0.90

what: Two decodings, one distinction worth knowing
title: Regime switching in time series: the hidden-Markov engine
text: viterbiPath() returns single jointly probable state sequence Markov prior; FFBS allocation draws give marginal posterior ztz_t, binderPartition() per-time MAP summarise. well-separated regimes agree; part exactly state genuinely ambiguous. FFBS draws populate clusterAllocation, label-free partition tools need adaptation: Plotting image(S) useful regime diagnostic: block structure along diagonal persistence made visible.
code: zv <- viterbiPath(fit) sum(diff(zv) != 0) + 1 #> [1] 15                             # 15 regime runs -- matches the truth  zmap <- apply(fit@clusterAllocation, 2,               function(v) as.integer(names(which.max(table(v))))) mean(zv == zmap) #> [1] 1                              # full agreement here bp <- binderPartition(fit) bp$K #> [1] 2 S <- psm(fit)                        # 300 x 300 posterior similarity

title: Regime switching in time series: the hidden-Markov engine
text: invariant draws array works engine (concentration parameter alpha exist dropped automatically): ppCheck() ppcData() also work, one honest nuance: replicated series drawn conditionally draw’s sampled state path, re-simulating Markov chain. right check “emissions fit, given regimes”; test transition structure , run-length comparison direct probe.
code: da <- drawsArray(fit)                # 2500 x 2 chains x {K, entropy} # bayesplot::mcmc_trace(da) pd <- ppcData(fit, ndraws = 20)      # list(y, yrep), yrep 20 x 300 # bayesplot::ppc_dens_overlay(pd$y, pd$yrep)

title: Regime switching in time series: the hidden-Markov engine
text: Order matters. Markov prior binds data order; shuffled data silently means ..d. mixture fitted expensively. State labels can switch, like mixture. relabel() handles component summaries; viterbiPath() partition tools label-free construction. -specifying K safe diluting. Extra states go (nearly) empty – engine’s tests assert corruption occurs – transitions barely-used states poorly informed; keep K number regimes can defend.

title: Multivariate mixture clustering with the DPM engine
text: component multivariate normal conjugate Normal-Inverse-Wishart cluster base measure (dmnorm(cov=) + dinvwish), data-scaled default. NIMBLE’s native conjugate CRP samplers used – nimix deliberately reimplement .

title: Multivariate mixture clustering with the DPM engine
text: 2022 data dominant structure clearly separated high-income / high-longevity cluster plus overlapping developing-country clusters: Overlapping components small weights expected DPM; report dominant components , hard partition needed, use MAP allocation:
code: fit <- nimixClust(Y, K_max = 8,                   mcmcControl = list(niter = 4000, nburnin = 1500),                   seed = 1) fit <- relabel(fit) summary(fit) #> modalK = 4 #>   component weight   mu_1   mu_2      # (log GDP pc, life expectancy) #> 3         3  0.309 10.363 80.302     # rich, long-lived cluster #> 4         4  0.444  8.087 69.402     # large developing cluster #> 1         1  0.204  8.570 71.788     # overlapping middle cluster #> 2         2  0.043  8.556 69.914     # small transient component alloc <- apply(fit@clusterAllocation, 2L, function(z)   as.integer(names(which.max(table(z))))) plot(Y, col = alloc, pch = 19,      main = "MAP cluster allocation")

title: Multivariate mixture clustering with the DPM engine
code: fit3 <- nimixClust(Y, K = 3, method = "fixedk",                    mcmcControl = list(niter = 4000, nburnin = 1500),                    seed = 1) summary(relabel(fit3))

title: Univariate mixture clustering with the DPM engine
text: method = "dpm" estimates number occupied components. K_max truncation level (computational headroom), prior belief K. 2022 data finds two-regime structure: posterior number clusters concentrates 2-3, usual DPM tail small transient components. fit warns truncation level reached non-negligible share iterations, increase K_max re-run – larger truncation adds headroom.
code: fit <- nimixClust(ly, K_max = 10,                   mcmcControl = list(niter = 4000, nburnin = 1500),                   seed = 1) fit <- relabel(fit)   # always relabel before summarising (label switching) summary(fit) #> modalK = 2 #>   weight mu_mean #> 1  0.554   7.966    # ~ US$ 2,900  (lower/middle-income regime) #> 2  0.446   9.964    # ~ US$ 21,000 (high-income regime)

title: Univariate mixture clustering with the DPM engine
text: Multiple chains dispersed starts cost almost nothing extra: compiled model reused across chains, summary() reports cross-chain split-Rhat / ESS label-invariant quantities.
code: fit3 <- nimixClust(ly, K_max = 10,                    mcmcControl = list(niter = 4000, nburnin = 1500,                                       nchains = 3),                    seed = 1) summary(relabel(fit3))

title: Mixtures of linear regressions
code: library(nimix) #> Loading required package: nimble #> nimble version 1.4.2 is loaded. #> For more information on NIMBLE and a User Manual, #> please visit https://R-nimble.org. #>  #> Attaching package: 'nimble' #> The following object is masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     simulate #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     declare data(wdi2022) df <- data.frame(life = wdi2022$life_exp,                  lgdp = log(wdi2022$gdp_pc)) plot(df$lgdp, df$life, xlab = "log GDP per capita",      ylab = "life expectancy", main = "Preston curve, 2022")

what: Does the Preston curve have latent regimes?
title: Mixtures of linear regressions
text: instructive result: log income DPM concentrates single regime – roughly four extra years life expectancy per log-dollar income. lesson nonparametric mixture invent regimes: one regression line (residual spread) explains data, posterior collapses . Estimated K always depends assumed component form – heterogeneity lives residuals, distinct slopes. Even two components offered (fixed K = 2), one simply empties:
code: fit <- nimixReg(life ~ lgdp, df, K_max = 6, method = "dpm",                 mcmcControl = list(niter = 5000, nburnin = 2000),                 seed = 1) fit <- relabel(fit) summary(fit) #> modalK = 1 #>   (Intercept)  lgdp #> 1      36.782 4.067      # life ~ 36.8 + 4.07 x log GDP pc fit2 <- nimixReg(life ~ lgdp, df, K = 2, method = "fixedk",                  mcmcControl = list(niter = 5000, nburnin = 2000),                  seed = 1) summary(relabel(fit2)) #> one occupied component; weight ~ 1 on the Preston line

title: Mixtures of linear regressions
text: genuinely regime-structured data call recovers . simulated benchmark (two latent groups slopes +2 / -2, mixed 80/20):
code: set.seed(107) n <- 250; x <- runif(n, -3, 3) grp <- rep(1:2, c(200, 50)) y <- ifelse(grp == 1, 2 * x, -2 * x) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.7) fs <- nimixReg(y ~ x, data.frame(y = y, x = x), K_max = 8,                mcmcControl = list(niter = 5000, nburnin = 2000), seed = 1) summary(relabel(fs))    # recovers slopes near +2 and -2, weights near 0.8/0.2

title: Mixtures of linear regressions
text: One number deserves caveat: component error variance s2. prior centred residual variance global OLS fit, ignores mixture — separated components measures spread components much spread within one, s2 comes large. bias largest exactly mixtures earn keep. 150 observations per component 1.0x overlapping components 4.7x well-separated ones; sample size runs ~9x 25 observations per component fades 1.0x 5000. safe direction — predictive intervals come wider, narrower — coefficients untouched. conservatism deliberate: also regularises splitting K -specified. Shrinking makes -specified fit occupy components . stays default — know within-component scale, say : simulated benchmark true s2 0.25, moved estimate 0.78 0.36 leaving slopes . override asks absolute value purpose: multiplier automatic scale ever mean “fraction quantity measures wrong thing” — knob rather statement. Tightening prior safe DPM, fixedk K right. safe K -specified: data K = 4 two true components, default kept two components occupied tenfold tighter prior filled third. unsure K, leave default alone use DPM.
code: nimixReg(y ~ x, df, K = 2, method = "fixedk",          prior = list(s2Guess = 0.3))   # your prior mean for s2

title: Mixtures of linear regressions
text: need scale right external knowledge, let first fit tell . allocation reliable even s2 — exactly makes work: costs second compile--run, default. Note caveat : reach K -specified.
code: fit1 <- relabel(nimixReg(y ~ x, df, K = 2, method = "fixedk"))  z    <- binderPartition(fit1)$partition          # MAP component per point cf   <- fit1@relabeled$summary Xm   <- model.matrix(y ~ x, df) res  <- df$y - rowSums(Xm * as.matrix(cf[z, c("(Intercept)", "x")])) s2hat <- sum(res^2) / (nrow(df) - 2 * ncol(Xm)) # within-component residual var  fit2 <- nimixReg(y ~ x, df, K = 2, method = "fixedk",                  prior = list(s2Guess = s2hat))

title: Mixtures of linear regressions
text: observations nested groups (regions, individuals, batches), shared group offset bg()∼N(0,τ2)b_{g()} \sim N(0, \tau^2) can added every component’s linear predictor random = ~ group: Two things worth knowing. offsets use sum--zero parameterisation, free bb component intercepts mean bb form translation ridge mixes badly (prototype measured cor = -0.979 minimum ESS 25 2500 draws; constraint restored ~205). reported b centred, component intercepts absorb group mean. exact conjugate coefficient sampler extended condition current offsets, keeps fit scale-equivariant predictors even random effect active. Reading tauRE needs one caution. estimates spread group offsets, groups spread lottery: twelve offsets drawn population σ=0.8\sigma = 0.8 realized standard deviation anywhere 0.5 1.1. apparent “miss” population value usually draw, estimator – judge tauRE groups actually . Groups need balanced across components. groups sitting almost entirely one component (case plausibly let offsets absorb component intercept), coefficients still separate cleanly offsets stay identified; test suite asserts .
code: # grouped data with 12 groups; component slopes +2 / -2 reg <- nimixReg(y ~ x, df, random = ~ g, K = 2, method = "fixedk",                 mcmcControl = list(niter = 3000, nburnin = 1200), seed = 1) sort(relabel(reg)@relabeled$summary$x) #> [1] -1.93  1.97                    # slopes recovered with the offset present mean(reg@mcmcSamples[, "tauRE"])       # the between-group SD, tau

title: Mixtures of linear regressions
text: random = ~ x | g lets slope x vary group well (intercept comes along, lme4’s (x|g)): obvious worry : components mixture regressions distinguished slopes, group-varying slopes might fight component identification. prototype pushed deliberately hard – group slope deviations spanning −2.05-2.05 1.431.43 component separation 4, one group’s effective slope landed exactly two components – held: group slopes recovered cor = 0.997 component allocation 95.3% accuracy. sum--zero logic applies, component slopes absorb mean(s_g) reported sRE centred. two sets offsets get independent priors; checked correlated truth (ρ=0.92\rho = 0.92), independent model still recovered correlation reappeared estimates anyway. observations per group prior work correlated prior matter . work heavy-tailed residuals — distribution = "studentt" accepts random = argument, often grouped data occasional outliers actually needs. Current scope: method = "fixedk", distribution = "normal" "studentt", one grouping factor, random intercept one random slope. families follow gated roadmap.
code: reg <- nimixReg(y ~ x, df, random = ~ x | g, K = 2, method = "fixedk",                 mcmcControl = list(niter = 3000, nburnin = 1200), seed = 1)

title: Heavy tails: Student-t vs Normal-Gamma components
code: library(nimix) #> Loading required package: nimble #> nimble version 1.4.2 is loaded. #> For more information on NIMBLE and a User Manual, #> please visit https://R-nimble.org. #>  #> Attaching package: 'nimble' #> The following object is masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     simulate #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     declare data(wdi2022) ly <- log(wdi2022$gdp_pc)

title: Heavy tails: Student-t vs Normal-Gamma components
text: Actual results dataset: agree two dominant income regimes. augmented path found one extra small component mixed somewhat slowly partition.
code: ctrl <- list(niter = 4000, nburnin = 1500) ft <- relabel(nimixClust(ly, K_max = 8, distribution = "studentt",                          mcmcControl = ctrl, seed = 1)) fg <- relabel(nimixClust(ly, K_max = 8, distribution = "normalgamma",                          mcmcControl = ctrl, seed = 1)) summary(ft); summary(fg) #> studentt     : modalK = 2, regimes at log-GDP 8.00 / 10.06, #>                ESS(#clusters) = 147, ~30 s #> normalgamma  : modalK = 3, dominant regimes 8.07 / 10.10 (+ small 10% #>                middle component), ESS(#clusters) = 116, ~38 s

title: Heavy tails: Student-t vs Normal-Gamma components
text: augmentation restores conjugacy, per-observation omega_i couple tightly partition, can slow mixing number clusters – penalty found consistently benchmarks mitigate replacing default random-walk sampler omega slice sampling (done automatically via spec’s sampler hook). Practical guidance: "studentt" sensible default heavy-tailed clustering; "normalgamma" worth trying Gibbs updates advantageous (e.g. larger n per component), omega_i posteriors useful: small values flag observations model treats outliers (robustness-weight interpretation). Always compare summary() ESS diagnostics data rather assuming one uniformly better.

title: Skew multivariate clustering with an estimated orthogonal factor
code: library(nimix)  rgen <- function(n, mu, Sigma, gamma, theta) {   m <- length(mu)   O <- orthogonalFactor(theta, m)          # Householder factor from angles   U <- chol(Sigma)   G <- matrix(gamma, n, m, byrow = TRUE)   W <- abs(matrix(rnorm(n * m), n, m))   eps <- ifelse(matrix(runif(n * m), n, m) < G^2 / (1 + G^2), W * G, -W / G)   sweep(eps %*% O %*% U, 2, mu, "+") }  set.seed(40) Sigma <- diag(3); Sigma[1, 2] <- Sigma[2, 1] <- 0.25 Y <- rbind(rgen(250, c(-4, -4, -4), Sigma, c(0.5, 2.0, 1.5), c( 0.30, -0.20,  0.40)),            rgen(250, c( 4,  4,  4), Sigma, c(2.0, 0.5, 0.7), c(-0.25,  0.35, -0.30)))

title: Skew multivariate clustering with an estimated orthogonal factor
text: user-facing name "skewnormal-mv-o" regardless dimension; fit routes data’s column count. two columns uses dedicated bivariate implementation; , general Householder parameterisation m(m−1)/2m(m-1)/2 angles. Partition location recovery exact:
code: fit <- relabel(nimixClust(Y, K = 2, method = "fixedk",                           distribution = "skewnormal-mv-o",                           mcmcControl = list(niter = 2500, nburnin = 1000),                           seed = 3)) class(fit@distSpec) #> [1] "SkewNormalMvOGenSpec" s <- fit@relabeled$summary sort(c(s$mu_1_mean)) #> [1] -3.85  4.21           # true -4, 4

title: Skew multivariate clustering with an estimated orthogonal factor
text: one thing trips people . rows AA can permuted sign-flipped without changing density – label switching, dimension index rather component index. nimix resolves mapping every posterior draw unique canonical representative (Ferreira & Steel’s identifiability restriction, applied post hoc rather sampling constraint). reported gamma O canonicalised, compare simulating values must canonicalise : Every draw found canonical representative:
code: c1 <- canonicaliseO(orthogonalFactor(c(0.30, -0.20, 0.40), 3), c(0.5, 2.0, 1.5)) round(c1$gamma, 2) #> [1] 0.50 0.50 0.67          # canonicalised truth, NOT the raw c(0.5, 2.0, 1.5)  round(c(s$gamma_1_mean[1], s$gamma_2_mean[1], s$gamma_3_mean[1]), 2) #> [1] 0.49 0.57 0.65          # estimate -- matches the canonicalised truth fit@relabeled$canonicalFraction #> [1] 1

what: Why restriction (8) is a canonicalisation, not a constraint
title: Skew multivariate clustering with an estimated orthogonal factor
text: Ferreira & Steel suggest confining Householder angles box OO identified. Testing directly shows work sampling constraint – small fraction angle draws box actually satisfy identifiability restriction, fraction shrinks fast dimension: Constraining sampler 7% (0.7%) slice prior mix badly. Applying restriction post-hoc canonicalisation instead – exactly one signed row permutation satisfies – keeps sampler unconstrained density invariant.
code: frac <- mean(replicate(500, {   th <- runif(3, -pi/2, pi/2)   nimix:::.restriction8(orthogonalFactor(th, 3)) })) frac #> [1] 0.068                   # ~7% at m = 3; ~0.7% at m = 4

title: Skew multivariate clustering with an estimated orthogonal factor
text: estimated-OO families m>2m > 2 marked experimental. Partition location recovery robust, : gamma O reported canonicalisation (compare canonicalised truth, ); O_mean elementwise posterior mean orthogonal – use O draws orthogonality matters; individual angles large-sample quantities, mirror modes angle likelihood multiply m; budget iterations fixed-OO families, especially MRF engine. heavy-tailed sibling, "skewistudent-mv-o", follows identical workflow extra per-margin degrees--freedom parameter nu.

title: Spatially constrained mixtures (MRF engine)
code: library(nimix) #> Loading required package: nimble #> nimble version 1.4.2 is loaded. #> For more information on NIMBLE and a User Manual, #> please visit https://R-nimble.org. #>  #> Attaching package: 'nimble' #> The following object is masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     simulate #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     declare data(usStates2023); data(usStateAdj) sw <- spatialWeights(usStateAdj) sw #> SpatialWeightSpec: 49 regions, 112 undirected edges #>   degree range: 1 - 8 | weights: binary (contiguity) neighborsOf(sw, "TN")     # Tennessee's famous 8 neighbours #> [1] "AL" "AR" "GA" "KY" "MS" "MO" "NC" "VA" summary(usStates2023$povertyRate) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>    7.30   10.30   11.90   12.27   13.70   18.90

title: Spatially constrained mixtures (MRF engine)
text: Potts normalising constant intractable. fixed beta (default prior$beta = 0.8) constant absorbed MCMC labels component parameters exact. Estimating beta (prior$estimateBeta = TRUE) uses classical pseudo-likelihood Metropolis update (Besag 1975) – approximation, slightly biased strong interactions; exchange-algorithm refinement possible future upgrade.

what: Two poverty regimes with spatial cohesion
title: Spatially constrained mixtures (MRF engine)
text: Actual results 2023 data: high-poverty regime spatially contiguous Southern belt (plus New Mexico, DC enclave inside belt) – exactly kind structure exchangeable mixture represent. fixedk fit data finds similar regime means agrees MRF allocation 82% states: spatial smoothing snaps boundary states neighbours’ regime.
code: y <- usStates2023$povertyRate fit <- nimixClust(y, K = 2, method = "mrf", spatialWeights = sw,                   prior = list(estimateBeta = TRUE),                   mcmcControl = list(niter = 4000, nburnin = 1500),                   seed = 1) fit <- relabel(fit) summary(fit) #> regimes: 11.4% vs 14.5% poverty (weights 0.73 / 0.27) #> beta   : posterior mean 1.22, P(beta > 0.2) = 1  (clear positive interaction) #> high-poverty regime (13 regions): #>   AL AR DC FL GA LA MS NC NM OK SC TN TX zMap <- apply(fit@clusterAllocation, 2L,               function(v) as.integer(names(which.max(table(v))))) split(usStates2023$postal, zMap)

title: Spatially constrained mixtures (MRF engine)
text: beta = 0 removes smoothing (labels independent given components); larger beta increases cohesion. estimated, summary() reports split-Rhat / ESS posterior mean beta. engine warns posterior beta piles near upper prior bound prior$betaMax (default 2): (near-)saturated field – raise betaMax interpret smoothing effectively maximal.

title: Spatially constrained mixtures (MRF engine)
text: field applies nimixReg(): regression coefficients cluster across adjacent regions (e.g. growth patterns across neighbouring areas). state-level pair fit collapses single national regime (pov ~ 144.6 - 11.8 log(income), strong income-poverty gradient): 49 regions one dominant relationship, model invent second regime – honesty Preston-curve vignette shows exchangeable case.
code: ## regimes in how income relates to poverty across neighbouring states df <- data.frame(pov = usStates2023$povertyRate,                  inc = log(usStates2023$medianIncome)) fr <- nimixReg(pov ~ inc, df, K = 2, method = "mrf", spatialWeights = sw,                prior = list(estimateBeta = TRUE),                mcmcControl = list(niter = 4000, nburnin = 1500), seed = 1) summary(relabel(fr))

title: Spatially constrained mixtures (MRF engine)
text: Estimated beta pseudo-likelihood approximation (see ); MRF engine currently supports Gaussian components (univariate, multivariate, univariate-response regression); heavy-tailed components spatial engine planned. Synthetic-lattice recovery benchmarks three model types ship inst/harness/run_recovery_suite.R (groups mrf, mrfmv, mrfreg).

what: Bayesian Mixture Clustering and Regression with NIMBLE
title: Bayesian Mixture Clustering and Regression with NIMBLE
text: nimix Bayesian mixture-modelling package built top NIMBLE. provides mixture clustering (univariate multivariate), mixtures regressions (univariate multivariate response), spatially coupled mixtures, extensible S4 DistributionSpec contract: new component families plug every inference engine without touching engine code. registry currently holds 32 component distributions.

title: Bayesian Mixture Clustering and Regression with NIMBLE
text: Classical — Normal (univariate multivariate), Student-t (), Normal-Gamma heavy-tailed (), Poisson, Binomial. Neo-normal univariate — MSNBurr, MSNBurr-IIa, GMSNBurr, exponential power pair sep/lep, Fernandez–Steel / Jones–Faddy skew families fssn, fossep, fsst, jfst. follow one skewness convention: alpha FS gamma, alpha > 1 skews right P(X > mu) = alpha^2 / (1 + alpha^2), uniformly across families. Ferreira–Steel skew multivariate — skew-Normal skew-independent- Student components ("skewnormal-mv", "skewistudent-mv"), orthogonal factor = OU either fixed estimated ("skewnormal-mv-o", "skewistudent-mv-o"). estimated-O families accept dimension m >= 2: m = 2 uses dedicated implementation, m > 2 general Householder parameterisation m(m-1)/2 angles FS’s identifiability restriction applied post-hoc canonicalisation. general-m variants experimental — partition location recovery robust, individual angles large-sample quantities reported gamma/O canonicalised (compare canonicalised truth; see ?canonicaliseO).

title: Bayesian Mixture Clustering and Regression with NIMBLE
text: Selected method: "dpm" — Dirichlet Process Mixture infers number components (NIMBLE’s CRP), bounded K_max. "fixedk" — finite mixture known K (including K = 1 baseline model comparison). "mrf" — spatially coupled allocations via Potts prior user-supplied adjacency (spatialWeights; gridAdjacency() builds regular grids). spacetimeAdjacency(W, nTime) expands graph time, giving spatio-temporal mixtures change: 5x5 grid 8 times overlapping components, plain mixture failed outright (accuracy 0.50), spatial graph reached 0.95, space-time graph 0.995. "hmm" — regime switching time: labels follow first-order Markov chain, state path marginalised forward algorithm (measured: min ESS/sec 456 vs 144 naive latent-state sampling), exact allocation draws recovered post hoc FFBS – relabel(), psm(), binderPartition() plots work unchanged. viterbiPath(fit) decodes jointly probable state sequence. Emissions (univariate, complete 13): Gaussian, Student-t (heavy tails), Poisson (count regimes), Binomial (regime-switching proportions, known size), nine neo-normal / skew families (MSNBurr, MSNBurr-IIa, GMSNBurr, FSSN, FSST, SEP, LEP, FOSSEP, JFST). normal-gamma excluded design: augmentation marginalised forward kernel exists avoid. nimixReg method gives Markov-switching regression (Hamilton 1989): coefficients error variance switch regime, rows time series rather exchangeable sample. Measured two regimes opposing slopes: intercepts 1.96/-1.96 2/-2, decoding 0.98. 32 families run dpm, fixedk mrf engines.

what: Sampler defaults that were measured, not assumed
title: Bayesian Mixture Clustering and Regression with NIMBLE
text: FixedK mixture regression uses exact Normal-Inverse-Gamma Gibbs update coefficients residual variances (NIMBLE’s conjugacy checker see mixture’s dynamic indexing). makes fit scale-equivariant predictors — measured slope discrepancy 1000× rescale fell 0.48 0.004 — raised coefficient ESS/second ~35×. DPM path already conjugate via NIMBLE’s CRP machinery. nine 3–4 parameter univariate families sample component’s parameters automated-factor-slice block (AF_slice; Tibbits et al. 2014). fssn, cor(mu, alpha) reaches −0.94, minimum ESS went 12 622 per 1500 draws; naive RW_block made worse.

title: Bayesian Mixture Clustering and Regression with NIMBLE
text: Component parameters: relabel() (post-hoc label-switching correction via label.switching package, conditioning modal number clusters), summary(), plot(), predict(). Partitions, label-free: psm() (posterior similarity matrix) binderPartition() (Dahl’s least-squares / expected-Binder-loss point partition) use every draw — relabelling, fixed K required. complement relabel(): answers “component parameters”, answer “observations belong together”. Internal validity, honest caveat: clusterValidity(fit) computes silhouette, Dunn Calinski-Harabasz Binder partition (via cluster/fpc, Suggests-). indices reward geometric separation mixtures density-based: overlapping components can exactly right model still score low – use compare partitions, judge model adequacy. Predictive checking: ppCheck() tail-probability summaries; posteriorPredict() (ppCheck(..., store_yrep = TRUE)) replicates . bayesplot, without dependency: drawsArray(fit) returns plain iterations × chains × parameters array bayesplot::mcmc_* accept natively, ppcData(fit) returns list(y, yrep) bayesplot::ppc_dens_overlay() friends. bayesplot sits Suggests . adaptors enforce one statistical guard: per-component draws refused relabel(), label switching R-hat raw muTilde trace looks valid means nothing — default "invariant" view (cluster count, allocation entropy, alpha) safe raw draws. Every plot(fit, type = ...) invisibly returns tidy data frame drew, can replot ggplot2/lattice/plotly without nimix carrying packages.

title: Bayesian Mixture Clustering and Regression with NIMBLE
text: nimix depends nimble, compiles model code, need working C/C++ toolchain (Rtools Windows, Xcode CLT macOS, build-essential Linux).
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("madsyair/nimix")  # or # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("madsyair/nimix")

title: Bayesian Mixture Clustering and Regression with NIMBLE
code: library(nimix)  ## Univariate clustering, number of components estimated (DPM) set.seed(1) y <- c(rnorm(100, -3), rnorm(100, 3)) fit <- nimixClust(y, K_max = 8,                   mcmcControl = list(niter = 2000, nburnin = 1000),                   verbose = FALSE) summary(fit) plot(fit, type = "K")  ## Label-free partition summary using every draw bp <- binderPartition(fit) table(bp$partition)  ## Skew multivariate clustering with an estimated orthogonal factor (m = 3) Y <- rbind(matrix(rnorm(300, -4), 100, 3), matrix(rnorm(300, 4), 100, 3)) sk <- relabel(nimixClust(Y, K = 2, method = "fixedk",                          distribution = "skewnormal-mv-o",                          mcmcControl = list(niter = 2500, nburnin = 1000)))  ## Regime-switching time series (hidden-Markov engine); data order = time order zt  <- cumsum(c(1, runif(299) < 0.07)) %% 2 + 1     # two persistent regimes yts <- rnorm(300, c(-2, 2)[zt], 0.7) hm  <- nimixClust(yts, K = 2, method = "hmm",                   mcmcControl = list(niter = 4000, nburnin = 1500)) viterbiPath(hm)                        # decoded regime per time point  ## Mixture of linear regressions, fixed number of regimes (finite mixture) x  <- runif(200, -3, 3) g  <- rep(1:2, each = 100) df <- data.frame(y = ifelse(g == 1, 2 * x, -2 * x) + rnorm(200, 0, 0.7), x = x) reg <- nimixReg(y ~ x, df, K = 2, method = "fixedk",                 mcmcControl = list(niter = 2000, nburnin = 1000),                 verbose = FALSE) summary(reg)  ## ... with random effects for grouped data (fixedk + normal) # nimixReg(y ~ x, df, random = ~ region, K = 2, method = "fixedk")      # intercept # nimixReg(y ~ x, df, random = ~ x | region, K = 2, method = "fixedk")  # + slope # ... add distribution = "studentt" for heavy-tailed residuals  ## Hand the fit to bayesplot (if installed) # bayesplot::mcmc_trace(drawsArray(fit))               # invariant functionals # pd <- ppcData(fit); bayesplot::ppc_dens_overlay(pd$y, pd$yrep)

title: Fitted nimix mixture result — FitResult-class
text: object FitResult. ... Passed underlying plot. type = "forecast", also h, newdata, lags, draws level, nimixForecast. x FitResult. y Ignored. type view draw. "K", "trace_raw",   "trace_relabeled", "density", "cluster"   "fitted" mixture views. "series", "regime" "forecast"   time-series views, method = "hmm" . density   plot regime-switching series wrong picture twice :   discards axis model , shows bimodal smear   story "series sat one regime, moved".   "series" draws data decoded regimes shaded   blocks (blocks, Markov chain models);   "regime" draws smoothed \(P(\mathrm{regime} \mid   \mathrm{data})\) time, honest companion   Viterbi path – bands mixed, decode guess;   "forecast" draws predictive fan past end series   (see nimixForecast fan can   mean). newdata Points evaluate: numeric vector (univariate clustering), matrix d columns (multivariate clustering), data frame predictors (regression). Defaults training data. maxDraws Integer cap number posterior draws used (default 500); draws thinned uniformly exceeded.

title: Fitted nimix mixture result — FitResult-class
text: Invisibly, tidy data frame exactly drawn (e.g.   iteration/component/value traces,   x/density predictive density), plot can   reproduced ggplot2 system without nimix depending   .

title: Fitted nimix mixture result — FitResult-class
text: show(FitResult): Compact display fitted result. summary(FitResult): Posterior summary (relabelled component estimates, posterior number clusters, mixing diagnostic). plot(x = FitResult, y = missing): Diagnostic result plots. predict(FitResult): Posterior predictive density new points. Returns posterior predictive density averaged MCMC draws, using occupied-cluster mixture draw (weights = cluster sizes / n). quantity label-invariant, relabelling required. multivariate fits density evaluated supplied rows newdata. regression fit (nimixReg) instead returns posterior predictive mean \(E[y \mid x]\) per row newdata (column .fitted). keep evaluation tractable draws subsampled maxDraws.

title: Fitted nimix mixture result — FitResult-class
text: mcmcSamples matrix monitored MCMC draws (iterations x parameters). Kposterior Integer vector: number occupied clusters per iteration. clusterAllocation Integer matrix (iterations x n) raw cluster indicators xi. paramTrace named list raw cluster-parameter traces (iterations x Kmax matrix). engineUsed Character scalar naming engine. distSpec DistributionSpec used. data data model fit . Kmax Integer truncation level. prior prior list used. relabeled list cache populated relabel (empty). mcmcControl MCMC control list actually used. diagnostics Multi-chain convergence diagnostics (Rhat, ESS) one chain run; otherwise single-chain summary. call matched call.

what: Finite-mixture engine with fixed, known K — FixedKEngine-class
title: Finite-mixture engine with fixed, known K — FixedKEngine-class
text: simplest engine: number components K fixed (inferred). Mixing weights symmetric Dirichlet prior observation categorical allocation. Chinese Restaurant Process, truncation considerations DPM apply, NIMBLE assigns conjugate samplers weights component parameters. Useful fast baseline K known assumed, classical model selection comparing fits across several values K.

title: Finite-mixture engine with fixed, known K — FixedKEngine-class
text: name Engine identifier, fixed "fixedk". dirichletConc Positive scalar concentration symmetric Dirichlet prior mixing weights (\(1\) uniform simplex; values 1 favour sparser weight vectors).

what: Hidden-Markov mixture engine (regime switching in time) — HMMEngine-class
title: Hidden-Markov mixture engine (regime switching in time) — HMMEngine-class
text: Component labels follow first-order Markov chain order observations (must therefore time order), instead independent: \(z_t \mid z_{t-1} \sim \mathrm{Cat}(P[z_{t-1}, ])\). state path marginalised likelihood forward algorithm, MCMC samples continuous parameters; allocation draws recovered exactly forward-filter backward-sampling (FFBS) per retained draw, makes every downstream tool (relabel, psm, binderPartition, plots) work unchanged.

text: Latent component labels follow Potts model neighbourhood graph SpatialWeightSpec instead independent across observations: neighbouring regions favour component, fixed interaction strength beta (Potts 1952; Besag 1974; spatially variant finite mixtures, Blekas et al. 2005). beta = 0 removes spatial smoothing. Bayesian estimation beta (hyperprior instead fixed value) planned later 1.x release.

title: Markov random field engine (spatially constrained finite mixture) — MRFEngine-class
code: MRFEngine(beta = 0.8, spatial, estimateBeta = FALSE, betaMax = 2)

title: Markov random field engine (spatially constrained finite mixture) — MRFEngine-class
text: beta Non-negative interaction strength (fixed value start). spatial SpatialWeightSpec. estimateBeta Logical; estimate beta pseudo-likelihood Metropolis. betaMax Upper bound uniform prior beta.

title: Markov random field engine (spatially constrained finite mixture) — MRFEngine-class
text: beta Non-negative spatial interaction strength: fixed value estimateBeta = FALSE, otherwise chain's starting value. spatial SpatialWeightSpec neighbourhood. estimateBeta Logical; update beta pseudo-likelihood Metropolis (Besag 1975) instead holding fixed. betaMax Upper bound uniform prior beta.

title: Markov random field engine (spatially constrained finite mixture) — MRFEngine-class
text: Besag, J. (1974). Spatial interaction statistical analysis lattice systems. JRSS B, 36(2), 192–236.

what: Skew multivariate independent-Student mixture components (Ferreira-Steel) — SkewIStudentMvSpec-class
title: Skew multivariate independent-Student mixture components (Ferreira-Steel) — SkewIStudentMvSpec-class
text: Heavy-tailed multivariate component: FS-skew Student-t margins per-dimension degrees freedom \(\nu_j\) (truncated 2), per-dimension skewness gamma, \(= \mathrm{chol}(\Sigma)\) upper triangular (orthogonal factor fixed identity; see SkewNormalMvSpec-class). gamma = 1 symmetric independent-Student; \(\nu \\infty\) recovers skew multivariate Normal. Non-conjugate.

title: Spatial neighbourhood structure for spatially constrained mixtures — SpatialWeightSpec-class
text: edges Integer matrix, one row per undirected edge endpoints < j, canonical column-major order (see getEdges). canonical representation since v1.5.0; dense matrix derived demand refused large graphs. edgeWeights Positive numeric weights, one per edge (1 binary contiguity). nNodes Integer number regions. regionIds Character vector length nNodes naming regions (defaults row names matrix, "region1", ... absent).

title: Build the NIMBLE model code for a (distribution, engine) pair — buildModelCode
text: spec DistributionSpec. engine EngineConfig. n Integer, number observations. L Integer, cluster-parameter truncation length (= K_max). ... Reserved methods. reSlope Logical; regression + FixedK method – TRUE (re = TRUE), also emit sum--zero random-slope offsets sRE plus tauSlope. re Logical; regression + FixedK method – TRUE, emit random-intercept variant model code (sum--zero b offsets plus tauRE).

title: Build the NIMBLE model code for a (distribution, engine) pair — buildModelCode
text: list elements code (nimbleCode object),   monitors (character vector), paramNodes   (named character vector mapping logical parameter names model nodes).

title: Build the NIMBLE model code for a (distribution, engine) pair — buildModelCode
text: buildModelCode(spec = FSSNUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): FSSN finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = FSSNUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): FSSN DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = FOSSEPUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): FOSSEP finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = FOSSEPUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): FOSSEP DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = FSSTUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): FSST finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = FSSTUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): FSST DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = PoissonRegSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Poisson GLM regression DPM code (log link). .glmRegREPriorLines(): Poisson GLM regression fixed-K code (log link). buildModelCode(spec = PoissonRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Poisson regression finite mixture (fixed K), optional random effect inside log link (GLMM). buildModelCode(spec = BinomialRegSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Binomial GLM regression DPM code (logit link). buildModelCode(spec = BinomialRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Binomial GLM regression fixed-K code (logit link). buildModelCode(spec = GMSNBurrUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): GMSNBurr finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = GMSNBurrUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): GMSNBurr DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = NormalRegSpec, engine = DPMEngine): DPM mixture--linear-regressions model code   (dCRP) conjugate Normal-Inverse-Gamma cluster prior constant   (CRP) gating. Builds NIMBLE code $$y_i \sim N(x_i^\top \beta_{\xi_i}, s^2_{\xi_i}), \quad       \xi_{1:n} \sim CRP(\alpha, n),$$ \(s^2_j \sim InvGamma(nu_0, s_0)\) \(\beta_j \mid s^2_j \sim N_p(b_0, s^2_j B_0)\). number predictors \(p\) design matrix \(X\) constants. buildModelCode(spec = StudentTRegSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Student-t regression DPM code (direct t density; scale enters precision tau = 1 / s2). buildModelCode(spec = StudentTRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Student-t regression fixed-K code. buildModelCode(spec = NormalGammaRegSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Normal-Gamma regression DPM code (scale mixture; conjugate coefficients conditional omega). buildModelCode(spec = NormalGammaRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Normal-Gamma regression fixed-K code. buildModelCode(spec = JFSTUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): JFST finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = JFSTUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): JFST DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = SEPUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): SEP finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = SEPUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): SEP DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = LEPUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): LEP finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = LEPUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): LEP DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = MSNBurrUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): MSNBurr finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = MSNBurr2aUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): MSNBurr-IIa finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = MSNBurrUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): MSNBurr DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = MSNBurr2aUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): MSNBurr-IIa DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = NormalMvRegSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Multivariate-response Normal regression DPM code. buildModelCode(spec = NormalMvRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Multivariate-response Normal regression fixed-K. buildModelCode(spec = StudentTMvRegSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Multivariate-response Student-t regression DPM code (direct multivariate-t kernel). buildModelCode(spec = StudentTMvRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Multivariate-response Student-t regression fixed-K. buildModelCode(spec = NormalGammaMvRegSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Multivariate-response Normal-Gamma regression DPM code (scale mixture; conjugate cluster updates). buildModelCode(spec = NormalGammaMvRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Multivariate-response Normal-Gamma regression fixed-K code. buildModelCode(spec = MSNBurrRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): MSNBurr regression finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = SEPRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): SEP regression finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = MSNBurr2aRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): MSNBurr-IIa regression finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = FSSNRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): FSSN regression finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = GMSNBurrRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): GMSNBurr regression finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = LEPRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): LEP regression finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = FSSTRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): FSST regression finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = FOSSEPRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): FOSSEP regression finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = JFSTRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): JFST regression finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = NormalMvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Multivariate Gaussian DPM model code (dCRP)   conjugate Normal-Inverse-Wishart cluster base measure. Builds NIMBLE code $$y_i \sim N_d(\mu_{\xi_i}, \Sigma_{\xi_i}), \quad       \xi_{1:n} \sim CRP(\alpha, n),$$ \(\Sigma_j \sim InvWishart(S_0, df_0)\) \(\mu_j \sim N_d(\mu_0, \Sigma_j / \kappa_0)\). dimension \(d\) constant index ranges 1:d expand model-build time. buildModelCode(spec = NormalGammaMvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Multivariate Normal-Gamma scale-mixture DPM code per-observation latent precision multiplier omega. buildModelCode(spec = NormalUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Univariate Gaussian DPM model code (dCRP). Builds NIMBLE code $$y_i \sim N(\mu_{\xi_i}, s^2_{\xi_i}), \quad       \xi_{1:n} \sim CRP(\alpha, n),$$ conjugate Normal-Inverse-Gamma cluster prior Gamma hyperprior \(\alpha\). Cluster-parameter vectors length L = K_max (NIMBLE's exact truncation; sampler stays proper long number occupied clusters strictly L). buildModelCode(spec = NormalGammaUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Normal-Gamma scale-mixture DPM model code (dCRP) per-observation latent precision multiplier omega. buildModelCode(spec = PoissonSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Poisson DPM model code (Gamma-Poisson conjugate). buildModelCode(spec = BinomialSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Binomial DPM model code (Beta-Binomial conjugate). buildModelCode(spec = SkewNormalMvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Skew-mv-Normal finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = SkewNormalMvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Skew-mv-Normal DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = SkewNormalMvOSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Skew-mv-Normal-O finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = SkewNormalMvOSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Skew-mv-Normal-O DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = SkewIStudentMvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Skew-mv-IStudent finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = SkewIStudentMvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Skew-mv-IStudent DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = SkewIStudentMvOSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Skew-mv-IStudent-O finite mixture (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = SkewIStudentMvOSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Skew-mv-IStudent-O DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = SkewNormalMvOGenSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): General-m skew-mv-Normal-O finite mixture. buildModelCode(spec = SkewNormalMvOGenSpec, engine = DPMEngine): General-m skew-mv-Normal-O DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = SkewIStudentMvOGenSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): General-m skew-mv-IStudent-O finite mixture. buildModelCode(spec = SkewIStudentMvOGenSpec, engine = DPMEngine): General-m skew-mv-IStudent-O DPM mixture. buildModelCode(spec = StudentTMvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Multivariate Student-t DPM code using user-defined dmvt_nimix kernel Normal-Inverse-Wishart cluster prior. buildModelCode(spec = StudentTUvSpec, engine = DPMEngine): Univariate Student-t DPM model code (dCRP). t density evaluated directly; df constant. buildModelCode(spec = NormalUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Univariate Gaussian finite-mixture code (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = NormalMvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Multivariate Gaussian finite-mixture code (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = NormalRegSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Mixture--linear-regressions finite-mixture code (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = StudentTUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Univariate Student-t finite-mixture code (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = NormalGammaUvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Univariate Normal-Gamma (scale-mixture Student-t) finite-mixture code (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = NormalGammaMvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Multivariate Normal-Gamma scale-mixture finite-mixture code (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = StudentTMvSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Multivariate Student-t finite-mixture code (fixed K) using user-defined dmvt_nimix kernel. buildModelCode(spec = PoissonSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Poisson finite-mixture code (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = BinomialSpec, engine = FixedKEngine): Binomial finite-mixture code (fixed K). buildModelCode(spec = NormalUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): Univariate Gaussian regime-switching HMM code (states marginalised forward algorithm; allocation node). buildModelCode(spec = StudentTUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): Univariate Student-t regime-switching HMM code (fixed df; states marginalised forward algorithm). buildModelCode(spec = MSNBurr2aUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): MSNBurr-IIa regime-switching HMM code. buildModelCode(spec = GMSNBurrUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): GMSNBurr (four-parameter, skewed) regime-switching HMM code. buildModelCode(spec = FSSNUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): FSSN (Ferreira-Steel skew normal) regime-switching HMM code. buildModelCode(spec = FSSTUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): FSST (Ferreira-Steel skew t) regime-switching HMM code – heavy-tailed skewed regimes . buildModelCode(spec = SEPUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): SEP (skew exponential power) regime-switching HMM code. buildModelCode(spec = LEPUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): LEP regime-switching HMM code. buildModelCode(spec = FOSSEPUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): FOSSEP regime-switching HMM code. buildModelCode(spec = JFSTUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): JFST (Jones-Faddy skew t) regime-switching HMM code. buildModelCode(spec = BinomialSpec, engine = HMMEngine): Binomial regime-switching HMM code (regime-specific success probabilities, known size). buildModelCode(spec = StudentTRegSpec, engine = HMMEngine): Markov-switching Student-t (heavy-tail) regression HMM code: location coefficients scale switch regime, df fixed hyperparameter. buildModelCode(spec = NormalGammaRegSpec, engine = HMMEngine): Markov-switching Normal-Gamma regression HMM code: Student-t marginal direct parameterisation, kernel. Defined explicitly two heavy-tail specs siblings NormalRegSpec, parent child (9.29/9.41). buildModelCode(spec = BinomialRegSpec, engine = HMMEngine): Markov-switching Binomial (proportion) regression HMM code: logit-link coefficients switch regime, known number trials size. buildModelCode(spec = PoissonRegSpec, engine = HMMEngine): Markov-switching Poisson (count) regression HMM code: log-link coefficients switch latent Markov regime, error-variance parameter. buildModelCode(spec = NormalRegSpec, engine = HMMEngine): Markov-switching regression HMM code: regression coefficients error variance switch latent first-order Markov regime (Hamilton 1989). buildModelCode(spec = MSNBurrUvSpec, engine = HMMEngine): MSNBurr regime-switching HMM code (skewed regimes; states marginalised forward algorithm). buildModelCode(spec = PoissonSpec, engine = HMMEngine): Poisson regime-switching HMM code (count regimes; states marginalised forward algorithm). buildModelCode(spec = DistributionSpec, engine = MRFEngine): Default MRF kernel family: family's fixed-K kernel Dirichlet-categorical label layer replaced joint Potts node (derived mechanically; see spatial design notes).

what: Canonical representative of an FS orthogonal factor — canonicaliseO
title: Canonical representative of an FS orthogonal factor — canonicaliseO
text: Maps (O, gamma, nu) unique signed row permutation satisfying Ferreira & Steel's identifiability restriction (8). Exactly one representative exists. Sigma invariant map.

title: Canonical representative of an FS orthogonal factor — canonicaliseO
code: canonicaliseO(O, gamma, nu = NULL)

title: Canonical representative of an FS orthogonal factor — canonicaliseO
text: O Orthogonal matrix determinant \((-1)^{m+1}\). gamma Positive skewness vector, length m. nu Optional positive degrees--freedom vector, length m.

title: Canonical representative of an FS orthogonal factor — canonicaliseO
text: list canonical O, gamma, (supplied)   nu. representative found (measure-zero event, e.g. zero   first column O), inputs returned unchanged   canonical = FALSE.

title: Canonical representative of an FS orthogonal factor — canonicaliseO
text: Ferreira & Steel (2007), Statistica Sinica 17, 505–529.

what: Profile the clusters of a fitted mixture — clusterProfile
title: Profile the clusters of a fitted mixture — clusterProfile
text: Assigns observation maximum posteriori (MAP) cluster – component occupies often across retained MCMC draws – summarises observed data within cluster: cluster size, proportion, per-variable within-cluster mean, standard deviation, median. characterises cluster terms data, complementing summary, reports fitted component parameters, plot(fit, type = "cluster"), shows partition visually.

title: Profile the clusters of a fitted mixture — clusterProfile
code: clusterProfile(fit, variables = NULL)

title: Profile the clusters of a fitted mixture — clusterProfile
text: fit FitResult nimixClust. Regression fits also accepted: response covariate profiled per regime. variables Optional character vector selecting data columns profile (multivariate / regression); default profiles .

title: Profile the clusters of a fitted mixture — clusterProfile
text: data.frame one row per occupied cluster columns   cluster, size, proportion, followed   <var>_mean, <var>_sd, <var>_median variable.

title: Profile the clusters of a fitted mixture — clusterProfile
text: Cluster ids recoded 1, 2, ... descending size (largest cluster first). mixture labels identified, data-side profile require relabel: summarises partition, partition label-invariant.

title: Profile the clusters of a fitted mixture — clusterProfile
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ fit <- nimixClust(y, K = 3, method = "fixedk") clusterProfile(fit) } # }

what: Internal validity indices for a fitted clustering — clusterValidity
title: Internal validity indices for a fitted clustering — clusterValidity
text: Computes standard internal cluster-validity indices – silhouette width, Dunn index, Calinski-Harabasz – point partition clustering fit. default partition [binderPartition()]'s least-squares partition, every posterior draw informs relabelling required.

title: Internal validity indices for a fitted clustering — clusterValidity
code: clusterValidity(   fit,   metrics = c("silhouette", "dunn", "ch"),   partition = NULL,   dist = NULL )

title: Internal validity indices for a fitted clustering — clusterValidity
text: fit [FitResult][FitResult-class] *clustering* fit ([nimixClust()]). Regression fits refused: distance-based indices meaning mixtures regressions. metrics Character vector, `"silhouette"`, `"dunn"`, `"ch"`. partition Optional integer vector cluster labels (length `n`). Default: `binderPartition(fit)$partition`. dist Optional `stats::dist` object. Default: Euclidean distance fitted data. Supply scaled non-Euclidean analyses.

title: Internal validity indices for a fitted clustering — clusterValidity
text: Named numeric vector one entry per requested metric.

title: Internal validity indices for a fitted clustering — clusterValidity
text: Internal indices reward *geometric* separation. mixture model *density*-based, fit genuinely overlapping components – often scientifically correct model – score low silhouette even [ppCheck()] says model reproduces data perfectly. Treat indices secondary lens (useful, e.g., comparing partitions different `K` equal footing), never model-adequacy verdict; job belongs [ppCheck()] [psm()]. full battery indices beyond three, call backends directly, e.g. `fpc::cluster.stats(d, part)` `clusterCrit::intCriteria(X, part, "")` – accept exactly `(partition, distance/data)` pair function assembles.

title: Internal validity indices for a fitted clustering — clusterValidity
code: # \donttest{ y <- c(rnorm(60, -3), rnorm(60, 3)) fit <- nimixClust(y, K_max = 6,                   mcmcControl = list(niter = 800, nburnin = 300),                   verbose = FALSE) clusterValidity(fit) #>   silhouette         dunn           ch  #>    0.7993590    0.1217575 1049.4718680  # }

Reserved methods.

title: Customise MCMC samplers for a component spec — customizeSamplers
text: customizeSamplers(DistributionSpec): Default: leave NIMBLE's samplers unchanged. customizeSamplers(NormalRegSpec): Replace RW samplers betaTilde s2Tilde exact Normal-Inverse-Gamma conditional (FixedK path ; DPM path already receives conjugate CRP wrapper). customizeSamplers(StudentTRegSpec): Student-t regression keeps NIMBLE's default samplers: inherited exact NIG Gibbs step valid Gaussian errors. customizeSamplers(NormalGammaRegSpec): Slice-sample latent precision multipliers. customizeSamplers(NormalGammaMvRegSpec): Slice-sample latent precision multipliers. customizeSamplers(NormalGammaMvSpec): Slice-sample latent precision multipliers. customizeSamplers(NormalGammaUvSpec): Slice-sample latent precision multipliers, mixes partition better default random walk. customizeSamplers(SkewNormalMvOSpec): Slice-sample Householder angles: FS likelihood theta bounded can multimodal near edge Theta^2, adaptive random walk mixes poorly. customizeSamplers(SkewIStudentMvOSpec): Slice-sample Householder angles. customizeSamplers(SkewNormalMvOGenSpec): Slice-sample Householder angles. customizeSamplers(SkewIStudentMvOGenSpec): Slice-sample Householder angles. customizeSamplers(MSNBurrUvSpec): AF_slice block (mu, sigma, alpha). customizeSamplers(MSNBurr2aUvSpec): AF_slice block (mu, sigma, alpha). customizeSamplers(GMSNBurrUvSpec): AF_slice block (mu, sigma, alpha, theta). customizeSamplers(FSSNUvSpec): AF_slice block (mu, sigma, alpha). customizeSamplers(FOSSEPUvSpec): AF_slice block (mu, sigma, alpha, theta). customizeSamplers(FSSTUvSpec): AF_slice block (mu, sigma, alpha, nu); truncated nu node poses problem slice sampling. customizeSamplers(JFSTUvSpec): AF_slice block (mu, sigma, alpha, theta). customizeSamplers(SEPUvSpec): AF_slice block (mu, sigma, nu). customizeSamplers(LEPUvSpec): AF_slice block (mu, sigma, nu).

title: Build a data-scaled default prior for a distribution — defaultPrior
text: defaultPrior(FSSNUvSpec): Data-scaled FSSN prior (log-normal skewness). defaultPrior(FOSSEPUvSpec): Data-scaled FOSSEP prior. defaultPrior(FSSTUvSpec): Data-scaled FSST prior. defaultPrior(PoissonRegSpec): g-prior coefficients. defaultPrior(BinomialRegSpec): g-prior coefficients; needs size. defaultPrior(GMSNBurrUvSpec): Data-scaled GMSNBurr prior. defaultPrior(NormalRegSpec): Data-scaled Normal-Inverse-Gamma g-prior   regression component. Requires design matrix control$X. Control overrides: g (g-prior factor; prior covariance beta s2 * g * solve(crossprod(X)), default g = n, unit-information prior) nu0 (InvGamma shape, default 3, must exceed 2 finite prior variance). defaultPrior(StudentTRegSpec): NIG g-prior plus fixed df (default 4, > 2). defaultPrior(NormalGammaRegSpec): NIG g-prior plus fixed df (default 4, > 2). defaultPrior(JFSTUvSpec): Data-scaled JFST prior (symmetric alpha/theta). defaultPrior(SEPUvSpec): Data-scaled SEP prior. defaultPrior(LEPUvSpec): Data-scaled LEP prior. defaultPrior(MSNBurrUvSpec): Data-scaled MSNBurr prior (location/scale/shape). defaultPrior(MSNBurr2aUvSpec): Data-scaled MSNBurr-IIa prior. defaultPrior(NormalMvRegSpec): Inverse-Wishart Sigma + matrix-normal   coefficients. Control overrides: g (coefficient g-prior scale, default   n) sigmaGuess, prior mean component's   residual covariance – positive scalar (read isotropic)   \(d \times d\) positive-definite matrix. sigmaGuess exists automatic reference   cov() global OLS residuals, carry   -component variation well within-component one.   multivariate face univariate problem behind   nimixReg's s2Guess. Measured two components   differing coefficients, isotropic within-covariance:   prior mean Sigma 22.4x truth trace, condition   number 40 truth circle – wrong size wrong shape. stays default anyway, reason clustering case :   df0 = d + 2 makes InverseWishart prior worth exactly one   observation every \(d\) (univariate InvGamma worth four),   fitted covariance came back 1.5x condition ~2   prior 22x. Reach sigmaGuess know   residual scale – often sigmaGuess = s isotropic   residuals variance s. "studentt"   "normalgamma" inherit . defaultPrior(StudentTMvRegSpec): Inverse-Wishart + matrix-normal plus fixed df. defaultPrior(NormalGammaMvRegSpec): Inverse-Wishart + matrix-normal plus fixed df. defaultPrior(MSNBurrRegSpec): MSNBurr regression prior. defaultPrior(SEPRegSpec): SEP regression prior. defaultPrior(MSNBurr2aRegSpec): MSNBurr-IIa regression prior (shares MSNBurr scale/shape prior). defaultPrior(FSSNRegSpec): FSSN regression prior (log-normal skewness). defaultPrior(GMSNBurrRegSpec): GMSNBurr regression prior. defaultPrior(LEPRegSpec): LEP regression prior. defaultPrior(FSSTRegSpec): FSST regression prior. defaultPrior(FOSSEPRegSpec): FOSSEP regression prior. defaultPrior(JFSTRegSpec): JFST regression prior. defaultPrior(NormalMvSpec): Data-scaled Normal-Inverse-Wishart prior (multivariate). Control overrides: cLoc (mean-dispersion multiplier; prior covariance mu Sigma / kappa0 kappa0 = 1 / cLoc^2, default cLoc = 2) df0 (inverse-Wishart degrees freedom, default d + 2, must exceed d + 1 finite, non-singular prior covariance empty components). sigmaGuess sets prior mean component's covariance directly: positive scalar (read isotropic) \(d \times d\) positive-definite matrix. default reference cov(data), global covariance, separated components inflated along direction separates – prior ellipse wrong shape, merely wrong size. Measured isotropic components separated along vector v: 37.6x along v 0.9x across , condition number 42.8 truth circle. InverseWishart default absorbs well (df0 = d + 2 makes prior worth exactly one observation every \(d\); fitted covariance came back 1.2x condition 1.4), stays. sigmaGuess callers know within-component shape – often sigmaGuess = s isotropic component variance s. defaultPrior(NormalGammaMvSpec): Normal-Inverse-Wishart prior plus fixed df (default 5, must exceed 2 finite component covariance). defaultPrior(NormalUvSpec): Data-scaled Normal-Inverse-Gamma prior. Control overrides: cLoc (location spread multiplier; prior sd mu ~ cLoc * sd(data), default 2), nu0 (InvGamma shape, default 3, must exceed 2 finite prior variance), concPrior. defaultPrior(NormalGammaUvSpec): Normal-Inverse-Gamma prior plus fixed df (degrees freedom, default 4, must exceed 2 finite component variance). defaultPrior(PoissonSpec): Data-scaled Gamma prior Poisson rate (E[lambda] ~= mean(y)). defaultPrior(BinomialSpec): Data-scaled Beta prior success probability. Requires number trials control$size. defaultPrior(SkewNormalMvSpec): Data-scaled skew-mv-Normal prior. defaultPrior(SkewNormalMvOSpec): Adds Householder angle bound mv prior. defaultPrior(SkewIStudentMvSpec): Data-scaled skew-mv-IStudent prior. defaultPrior(SkewIStudentMvOSpec): Adds Householder angle bound. defaultPrior(SkewNormalMvOGenSpec): Adds Householder angle box. defaultPrior(SkewIStudentMvOGenSpec): Adds Householder angle box. defaultPrior(StudentTMvSpec): Normal-Inverse-Wishart prior plus fixed df (default 5, must exceed 2 finite component covariance). defaultPrior(StudentTUvSpec): Data-scaled Normal location / Gamma precision prior   Student-t component. Control overrides: cLoc (location spread multiplier, default 2), df (degrees freedom, fixed hyperparameter, default 4, must exceed 2 finite component variance).

"components" Per-component parameters     relabel. Refused relabel() run:     label switching, muTilde[1] names different components     different chains, R-hat computed looks valid meaning     nothing. relabelling conditions modal cluster count,     chains lose equal lengths; draws therefore pooled single     chain, suitable posterior density/interval plots     cross-chain R-hat.

title: Fernandez-Osiewalski-Steel Skew Exponential Power Distribution — fossep-distribution
text: x, q Numeric vector quantiles. mu Numeric. Location parameter (default = 0). sigma Numeric. Scale parameter, must positive (default = 1). alpha Numeric. Skewness parameter, must positive (default = 2). Follows Fernandez-Steel convention shared skew families nimix: alpha FS skewness \(\gamma\), \(P(X > \mu) = \alpha^2/(1 + \alpha^2)\) alpha > 1 skews right. theta Numeric. Kurtosis/exponential-power parameter, must positive (default = 2). log, log.p Logical. TRUE, probabilities/densities given log. Default FALSE. lower.tail Logical. TRUE (default), probabilities \(P[X \le x]\), otherwise \(P[X > x]\). p Numeric vector probabilities. n Integer. Number observations generate.

title: Fernandez-Osiewalski-Steel Skew Exponential Power Distribution — fossep-distribution
text: dfossep gives density, pfossep gives distribution function, qfossep gives quantile function, rfossep generates random deviates.

title: Fernandez-Osiewalski-Steel Skew Exponential Power Distribution — fossep-distribution
text: Fernandez-Osiewalski-Steel Skew Exponential Power distribution parameters \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(\alpha\), \(\theta\) density: $$f(y|\mu,\sigma,\alpha,\theta) = \frac{c}{\sigma}   \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} |\alpha z|^\theta\right) \quad\text{} y < \mu$$ $$f(y|\mu,\sigma,\alpha,\theta) = \frac{c}{\sigma}   \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} |z/\alpha|^\theta\right) \quad\text{} y \ge \mu$$ \(z = (y - \mu)/\sigma\), \(c = \frac{\alpha\theta}{(1+\alpha^2)\,2^{1/\theta}\,\Gamma(1/\theta)}\). \(\theta = 2\), reduces Fernandez-Steel Skew Normal (FSSN).

title: Fernandez-Osiewalski-Steel Skew Exponential Power Distribution — fossep-distribution
text: Fernandez, C., Osiewalski, J., & Steel, M. F. (1995). Modeling inference v-spherical distributions. Journal American Statistical Association, 90(432), pp 1331-1340. Rigby, R. ., Stasinopoulos, M. D., Heller, G. Z., & De Bastiani, F. (2019). Distributions Modeling Location, Scale, Shape: Using GAMLSS R. CRC Press.

title: Fernandez-Osiewalski-Steel Skew Exponential Power Distribution — fossep-distribution
code: dfossep(0, mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 2, theta = 2) #> [1] 0.3191538  x <- seq(-5, 5, by = 0.1) plot(x, dfossep(x, alpha = 0.5, theta = 1.5), type = "l",      ylab = "Density", main = "FOSSEP Densities") lines(x, dfossep(x, alpha = 2, theta = 3), col = "red")   pfossep(c(-1, 0, 1), mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 2, theta = 2) #> [1] 0.009100053 0.200000000 0.506339938 qfossep(c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975), mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 2, theta = 2) #> [1] -0.7670603  0.9775528  4.3077494  set.seed(123) r <- rfossep(1000, mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 2, theta = 2) hist(r, breaks = 30, freq = FALSE, main = "FOSSEP Random Samples") curve(dfossep(x, alpha = 2, theta = 2), add = TRUE, col = "red", lwd = 2)

Follows Fernandez-Steel convention shared skew families nimix: alpha FS skewness \(\gamma\), \(P(X > \mu) = \alpha^2/(1 + \alpha^2)\) alpha > 1 skews right. \(\alpha = 1\) gives standard normal distribution. log, log.p Logical. title: Fernandez-Steel Skew Normal Distribution — fssn-distribution
text: Fernandez-Steel Skew Normal distribution special case FOSSEP distribution \(\theta = 2\). applies Fernandez-Steel skewing mechanism standard normal kernel. $$f(y|\mu,\sigma,\alpha) = \frac{2}{\sigma(\alpha + 1/\alpha)}   \phi(z/\alpha) \quad\text{} y < \mu$$ $$f(y|\mu,\sigma,\alpha) = \frac{2}{\sigma(\alpha + 1/\alpha)}   \phi(\alpha z) \quad\text{} y \ge \mu$$ \(z = (y - \mu)/\sigma\) \(\phi\) standard normal density.

title: Fernandez-Steel Skew Normal Distribution — fssn-distribution
text: Fernandez, C., Osiewalski, J., & Steel, M. F. (1995). Modeling inference v-spherical distributions. Journal American Statistical Association, 90(432), pp 1331-1340.

title: Fernandez-Steel Skew Normal Distribution — fssn-distribution
code: dfssn(0, mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 1) #> [1] 0.3989423  x <- seq(-5, 5, by = 0.1) plot(x, dfssn(x, alpha = 0.5), type = "l",      ylab = "Density", main = "FSSN Densities") lines(x, dfssn(x, alpha = 1), col = "red") lines(x, dfssn(x, alpha = 2), col = "blue")   pfssn(c(-1, 0, 1), mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 2) #> [1] 0.009100053 0.200000000 0.506339938 qfssn(c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975), mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 2) #> [1] -0.7670603  0.9775528  4.3077494  set.seed(123) r <- rfssn(1000, mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 2) hist(r, breaks = 30, freq = FALSE, main = "FSSN Random Samples") curve(dfssn(x, alpha = 2), add = TRUE, col = "red", lwd = 2)

title: Fernandez-Steel Skew t Distribution — fsst-distribution
text:
Fernandez-Steel Skew t distribution applies Fernandez-Steel skewing mechanism Student-t kernel \(
u\) degrees freedom. $$f(y|\mu,\sigma,\alpha,
u) =    rac{2}{\sigma(\alpha + 1/\alpha)} t_
u(z/\alpha) \quad	ext{} y < \mu$$ $$f(y|\mu,\sigma,\alpha,
u) =    rac{2}{\sigma(\alpha + 1/\alpha)} t_
u(\alpha z) \quad	ext{} y \ge \mu$$ \(z = (y - \mu)/\sigma\) \(t_
u\) standard Student-t density \(
u\) degrees freedom. mean exists \(
u > 1\) variance exists \(
u > 2\).

First shape parameter, must positive (default = 1). theta Numeric. Second shape parameter, must positive (default = 1). log, log.p Logical. TRUE, probabilities/densities given log. title: GMSNBurr Distribution — gmsnburr-distribution
text: GMSNBurr distribution parameters \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(\alpha\), \(\theta\) probability density function: $$f(x|\mu,\sigma,\alpha,\theta) = \frac{\omega}{B(\alpha,\beta)\sigma}   \left(\frac{\theta}{\alpha}\right)^\theta   \exp\left(-\theta\omega\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)   \left(1 + \frac{\theta}{\alpha}   \exp\left(-\omega\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)\right)^{-(\alpha+\theta)}$$ Special cases: \(\alpha = \theta\): Symmetric distribution \(\alpha < \theta\): Left-skewed \(\alpha > \theta\): Right-skewed \(\theta = 1\): Reduces MSNBurr \(\alpha = 1\): Reduces MSNBurr-IIa \(\alpha = \theta \\infty\): Converges \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\)

title: GMSNBurr Distribution — gmsnburr-distribution
text: Choir, . S. (2020). New Neo-Normal Distributions Properties. Dissertation. Institut Teknologi Sepuluh Nopember. Iriawan, N. (2000). Computationally Intensive Approaches Inference Neo-Normal Linear Models. PhD Thesis, Curtin University Technology.

title: GMSNBurr Distribution — gmsnburr-distribution

title: Jones-Faddy Skew-t Distribution — jfst-distribution

Left tail parameter (> 0), Jones-Faddy \(\) parameter (default = 3). \(\alpha = \theta\) gives symmetry. theta Numeric. Right tail parameter (> 0), Jones-Faddy \(b\) parameter (default = 3). \(\alpha = \theta\) gives symmetry. log, log.p Logical. TRUE, probabilities/densities given log. text: djfst gives density, pjfst gives distribution function, qjfst gives quantile function, rjfst generates random deviates.

title: Jones-Faddy Skew-t Distribution — jfst-distribution
text: Jones-Faddy Skew-t distribution parameters \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(\alpha\) (left tail), \(\theta\) (right tail) density: $$f(y|\mu,\sigma,\alpha,\theta) = \frac{c}{\sigma}   \left[1 + \frac{z}{\sqrt{\alpha+\theta+z^2}}\right]^{\alpha+0.5}   \left[1 - \frac{z}{\sqrt{\alpha+\theta+z^2}}\right]^{\theta+0.5}$$ \(z = (y-\mu)/\sigma\), \(c = \left[2^{\alpha+\theta-1} \sqrt{\alpha+\theta} \,   B(\alpha,\theta)\right]^{-1}\). \(\alpha < \theta\): left-skewed (heavier right tail). \(\alpha > \theta\): right-skewed (heavier left tail). \(\alpha = \theta\): Student-$t$ distribution (symmetric, \(\nu = 2\alpha\)). \(\alpha = \theta\), JFST reduces Student-$t$ distribution \(\nu = 2\alpha\) degrees freedom. \(\alpha = \theta \\infty\), JFST approaches Normal distribution. Moments exist certain conditions \(\alpha\) \(\theta\) met: mean requires \(\alpha,\theta > 0.5\), variance requires \(\alpha,\theta > 1\), skewness requires \(\alpha,\theta > 1.5\), kurtosis requires \(\alpha,\theta > 2\).

title: Jones-Faddy Skew-t Distribution — jfst-distribution
text: Jones, M.C. Faddy, M.J. (2003). skew extension t distribution, applications. Journal Royal Statistical Society, Series B, 65, pp 159-174.

title: Jones-Faddy Skew-t Distribution — jfst-distribution
code: djfst(0, mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 3, theta = 3) #> [1] 0.3827328  x <- seq(-5, 5, by = 0.1) plot(x, djfst(x, alpha = 3, theta = 3), type = "l",      ylab = "Density", main = "JFST Densities") lines(x, djfst(x, alpha = 5, theta = 1), col = "red") lines(x, djfst(x, alpha = 1, theta = 5), col = "blue")   pjfst(c(-2, 0, 2), mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 3, theta = 3) #> [1] 0.04621316 0.50000000 0.95378684 qjfst(c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975), mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 3, theta = 3) #> [1] -2.446912  0.000000  2.446912  set.seed(123) r <- rjfst(1000, mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 3, theta = 3) hist(r, breaks = 30, freq = FALSE, main = "JFST Random Samples") curve(djfst(x, alpha = 3, theta = 3), add = TRUE, col = "red", lwd = 2)

title: MSNBurr Distribution — msnburr-distribution
text: MSNBurr distribution parameters \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(\alpha\) probability density function: $$f(x|\mu,\sigma,\alpha) = \frac{\omega}{\sigma}   \exp\left(-\omega\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)   \left(1 + \frac{1}{\alpha}\exp\left(-\omega\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)\right)^{-(\alpha+1)}$$ \(-\infty < x < \infty\), \(-\infty < \mu < \infty\), \(\sigma > 0\), \(\alpha > 0\), \(\omega\) normalizing constant: $$\omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}   \left(1 + \frac{1}{\alpha}\right)^{\alpha+1}$$ MSNBurr special case GMSNBurr distribution \(\theta = 1\). left-skewed (negative skewness).

title: MSNBurr-IIa Distribution — msnburr2a-distribution
text: dmsnburr2a gives density, pmsnburr2a gives distribution function, qmsnburr2a gives quantile function, rmsnburr2a generates random deviates.

title: MSNBurr-IIa Distribution — msnburr2a-distribution
text: MSNBurr-IIa distribution parameters \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(\alpha\) probability density function: $$f(x|\mu,\sigma,\alpha) = \frac{\omega}{\sigma}   \exp\left(\omega\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)   \left(1 + \frac{1}{\alpha}\exp\left(\omega\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)\right)^{-(\alpha+1)}$$ \(\omega\) normalizing constant MSNBurr. MSNBurr-IIa special case GMSNBurr distribution \(\alpha_{\text{gmsnburr}} = 1\) \(\beta_{\text{gmsnburr}} = \alpha\). right-skewed (positive skewness).

title: MSNBurr-IIa Distribution — msnburr2a-distribution
text: Choir, . S. (2020). New Neo-Normal Distributions Properties. Dissertation. Institut Teknologi Sepuluh Nopember.

title: MSNBurr-IIa Distribution — msnburr2a-distribution
code: dmsnburr2a(0, mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 2) #> [1] 0.3989423  x <- seq(-3, 10, by = 0.1) plot(x, dmsnburr2a(x, alpha = 0.1), type = "l",      ylab = "Density", main = "MSNBurr-IIa Densities") lines(x, dmsnburr2a(x, alpha = 0.5), col = "red") lines(x, dmsnburr2a(x, alpha = 1), col = "blue") lines(x, dmsnburr2a(x, alpha = 5), col = "forestgreen")   pmsnburr2a(c(-2, 0, 2), mu = 0, sigma = 1, alpha = 1) #> [1] 0.0394854 0.5000000 0.9605146 qmsnburr2a(c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975), alpha = 1) #> [1] -2.295797  0.000000  2.295797  set.seed(123) r <- rmsnburr2a(1000) hist(r, breaks = 30, freq = FALSE, main = "MSNBurr-IIa Random Samples") curve(dmsnburr2a(x), add = TRUE, col = "red", lwd = 2)
